items.title: US-Iran ceasefire fractures: IRGC fires on F-35, US strikes back — markets bid anyway
items.idea: Overnight the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps fired on a US F-35 fighter jet, triggering US self-defense strikes on Iranian missile launch sites and boats attempting to place mines. WTI bounced from below $90 to $93/barrel. NYT reports US and Iran agreed in principle to reopen the Strait of Hormuz, with Iran proposing to open it 30 days after signing. 33 ships already passed through with Iran's permission over the weekend. Rubio says the deal is a few days away. The uranium stockpile remains the key sticking point. Markets completely ignored the military escalation: SPX futures +0.7%, NDX +1.2%, 10-yr yield down 9bps to below 4.5%. Seventh consecutive week of risk-on signal.
items.angle: Markets are right to separate military incidents from the diplomatic trajectory. The 30-day post-signing Hormuz opening timeline Iran proposed is the real clock to watch, not the individual military exchanges. When the formal deal is signed, the oil repricing is sharp and immediate.
items.tweetA: US F-35 fired on by IRGC. US strikes Iranian missile sites and mine-laying boats. Rubio says deal in a few days. 33 ships already crossing Hormuz. SPX +0.7%, yields down 4th session. The military noise is not the signal. The 30-day open-Hormuz window Iran agreed to — that IS the signal. $USO $XLE
items.tweetB: Everyone is watching for the Iran deal like it is a binary event. It is not. Iran said Hormuz opens 30 days post-signing. Oil reprices on signing, not on traffic. The uranium stockpile is the actual sticking point Rubio is not talking about publicly. The 7th consecutive week of equity risk-on tells you what the market believes.

charts.desc: 8 charts: SPX futures, 10yr yield, DXY, WTI crude, Gold, Copper, NatGas, Bitcoin

items.title: JPMorgan models Brent at $60 long-term if Hormuz fully reopens — sub-$90 WTI reopens June cut
items.idea: JPMorgan running a base case of Brent at $60/barrel if Hormuz tensions fully ease. Brent fell 7% Monday to $96, WTI shed over 6% to $90. Sub-$90 WTI directly reopens the June FOMC rate-cut conversation in a way nothing else this month has managed. A $60 Brent scenario represents approximately 37% downside from current $96 — the most powerful single disinflationary impulse available to the global economy right now.
items.angle: The market is pricing a partial deal, not a full Hormuz reopening. If Brent actually reprices toward $60, every rate-sensitive sector gets a simultaneous tailwind. $IWM could be the surprise winner of a full Hormuz deal.
items.tweetA: JPMorgan base case: Brent at $60 if Hormuz fully reopens. We are at $96. That is a $36 downside scenario. Sub-$90 WTI reopens the June cut debate. A full Hormuz deal may be the most powerful rate-cut catalyst of 2026, and it has nothing to do with the Fed dot plot. $USO $IWM $TLT
items.tweetB: Nobody is modeling what a $60 Brent scenario does to Core PCE. Energy contributes roughly 7% of the index weight. A 37% drop in oil is a 2-3 point disinflationary impulse. Powell would have cover for 3 cuts by year-end. The Hormuz deal does not just close a strait. It changes the macro regime.

items.title: Boockvar: Iran deal = commodity price pause, not end of bull market; EU fertilizer stockpiling; US rig count +10 to 425
items.idea: Even if the Iran deal closes and oil pulls back, Boockvar argues we are in a full fledged commodity bull market driven by global strategic stockpiling of all key materials over the coming years. The EU is actively exploring fertilizer stockpiling as food crisis prevention. US crude oil rig count jumped 10 to 425 (most since last July) but still 200 rigs below the November 2022 peak. He remains long physical precious metals, mining stocks, oil/gas, fertilizer, and uranium.
items.angle: The commodities thesis is not the Iran trade. It is the decade-long strategic stockpiling cycle. EU fertilizer reserves, US SPR rebuild, China rare earth accumulation are all driven by the same national security logic. The pullback on an Iran deal is the entry point, not the exit point.
items.tweetA: Oil pulls back on Iran deal talk. Boockvar is not celebrating. His thesis: global strategic stockpiling of ALL key materials is just beginning. EU fertilizer reserves, SPR rebuild, rare earth hoarding. US rig count at 425, most since July — but still 200 rigs below Nov 2022. The Iran deal is the buy-the-dip in commodities, not the end of the cycle. $CF $MOS $NTR
items.tweetB: The EU is stockpiling fertilizers because the food system is more fragile than people admit. Same logic as US SPR rebuild, China rare earth accumulation, Japan LNG reserves. Commodity bull markets end when supply convincingly outpaces demand. At 425 US rigs vs 625 peak, supply has not caught up. Full fledged commodity bull market — Boockvar.

items.title: Russia tells US citizens to leave Kyiv — standard pre-escalation warning protocol
items.idea: Russia formally warned US citizens to evacuate Kyiv, a step that in Russia's playbook typically precedes significant military escalation. This happens the same week as US-Iran peace talks appear to be making progress. The dual-theater geopolitical situation creates a split commodity and defense portfolio dynamic: deescalation in the Middle East may be enabling escalation in Eastern Europe.
items.angle: Russia's Kyiv evacuation warning is the kind of signal that has preceded major escalations in historical playbooks. The Iran-Ukraine simultaneity is underpriced: if Middle East peace releases pressure on the oil trade, Ukraine escalation puts a floor under European energy and defense names.
items.tweetA: Russia just told US citizens to leave Kyiv. This is Russia's standard pre-escalation signaling. It drops the same week as US-Iran peace optimism. Two theaters. Two opposite directions. Oil falls on Hormuz deal. European defense and energy catches a bid on Ukraine escalation. The diversification this implies is not being discussed. $LMT $HII $SAAB
items.tweetB: Everyone is focused on Hormuz. The Russia-Ukraine story is getting quieter — precisely when Russia is signaling louder. Evacuation warnings for US nationals in a capital city are not routine diplomatic noise. If both Middle East and Ukraine are live simultaneously, the defense complex run is early innings.

items.title: S&P at 7,473 ATH — 19% rally since April 13 gamma regime shift; $49B net GEX cushion 125pts above flip
items.idea: SPX closed Friday at 7,473, seven consecutive weeks of risk-on signal. The gamma flip sits at 7,348 — dealers operating 125 points above it in positive gamma territory with net GEX at $49.38 billion. The regime shift from negative to positive gamma on April 13 (when SPX was at approximately 6,275) is the structural anchor for the entire rally — a 19% move since the flip. The flip line has risen steadily alongside SPX, indicating the gamma cushion is structurally sound.
items.angle: Positive gamma means dealers mechanically buy weakness and sell strength — an embedded shock absorber at record highs. Until SPX breaks below 7,348 and gamma flips negative, this rally has a structural buyer. The risk comes from Thursday's macro pile-up, not from the tape itself.
items.tweetA: The S&P rallied 19% since April 13. Most explain this with fundamentals. The structural explanation: the gamma regime flipped positive at 6,275 that day. Dealers have been mechanical buyers of every dip since, with $49B in net GEX cushioning the tape. The flip line is at 7,348. Below that, the dynamic reverses. $SPX
items.tweetB: Seven consecutive weeks of risk-on signal. Net GEX $49 billion. Gamma flip 125 points below spot. The market is not irrationally exuberant — it is mechanically cushioned. Dealer positioning explains the low realized vol at ATH better than any macro narrative does. Different things entirely.

charts.desc: Gamma flip chart + sector momentum heatmap — CHART WORTH SAVING

items.title: Thursday May 28 macro pile-up: GDP + Core PCE + Durable Goods + Claims all same morning; +/-1.36% implied
items.idea: Thursday May 28 is the heaviest macro day of the week — GDP preliminary (prior 2.0%), Core PCE (prior 0.7%), PCE Price Index, Initial Jobless Claims, Durable Goods, Personal Income, Personal Spending, New Home Sales, and Building Permits all land the same morning. SPX IV at that expiration implies plus or minus 1.36%. A Core PCE print above 0.3% m/m would reinforce higher-for-longer and could trigger systematic fund mechanical selling. Thursday also has Costco, Dell, MongoDB, Autodesk, Okta competing for tape control simultaneously.
items.angle: Thursday is the real risk event of the week. Core PCE above 0.3% with SPX at ATH and 7 weeks of risk-on = maximum vulnerability for a technical selloff. Hot PCE + heavy earnings + potential systematic vol spike on the same morning is the setup bears have been waiting for.
items.tweetA: Nobody is talking about Thursday. GDP + Core PCE + Durable Goods + Claims + Building Permits + Costco + Dell + MongoDB all land the same morning May 28. The Gamma Report: heaviest macro day by a wide margin. Implied plus or minus 1.36%. If Core PCE prints above 0.3% m/m, systematic funds start selling. $VIX will move.
items.tweetB: Core PCE is still the Fed's preferred gauge. A Thursday print above 0.3% m/m hits at the exact moment equity positioning is most crowded: 7 weeks risk-on, SPX at ATH, $49B in dealer gamma. Hot PCE plus heavy earnings plus systematic selling is the correction setup markets have been ignoring.

items.title: S&P +29% from trough matches 1998 LTCM exactly — but rates and dollar are NOT rhyming, and that is the signal
items.idea: Paper Alfa aligned six data series from the 1998 LTCM crisis against the current 2025-2026 analog. S&P is up exactly 29% from the April 2025 trough — identical to the 1998 equivalent point in the recovery. But the divergence is the real insight: in 1998, the dollar was strengthening and the Fed cut preemptively. Today the dollar is weakening and the Fed is NOT cutting. In 1999-2000, after the 1998 equity melt-up, the Fed reversed hard and the Nasdaq lost 80%. The divergences in rates and the dollar are the signposts to watch.
items.angle: If the equity melt-up plays out without the rate-cut fuel that made 1998-99 run further, the runway is shorter and the eventual reversal is larger. The analogues that match on equity and diverge on macro are the dangerous ones.
items.tweetA: S&P is up 29% from the trough. In 1998, at the same point in the LTCM recovery, it was also up 29%. Spooky. But Paper Alfa's insight is where the analogs STOP rhyming: rates and the dollar. In 1998 the Fed cut. Today they are not. What happened in 1999-2000 after that melt-up? Nasdaq down 80%. The rhyme has a very dark ending.
items.tweetB: The 1998 LTCM analog is the most useful framework in markets right now. Equity behavior is identical to the current cycle. But the Fed is on a completely different path — in 1998, preemptive cuts fueled the last leg. This time there are no cuts in the tank. If the analog is right, the runway is shorter and the reversal is more violent.

charts.desc: Six side-by-side 1998 vs 2025 chart comparisons: S&P, rates, dollar, Fed path — CHART WORTH SAVING

items.title: US consumer sentiment near all-time lows — Americans feel worse about economy than during COVID
items.idea: US consumer sentiment has fallen near all-time lows, with survey data showing Americans are more pessimistic about economic conditions than during the COVID pandemic. This sits against a backdrop of SPX at all-time highs and 7 consecutive weeks of equity risk-on signal. Conference Board Consumer Confidence drops today, with the Gamma Report flagging a print below 90 as a flashing yellow light at current equity positioning.
items.angle: The widest sentiment divergence in modern history: ATH equities + pandemic-level consumer despair. The top 10% of households own 90%+ of equities. The bottom 90% feel the inflation, job anxiety, and credit stress. Both can simultaneously be true without contradiction.
items.tweetA: Consumer sentiment is at levels worse than COVID. S&P 500 is at all-time highs. Both true in May 2026. The explanation: top 10% own 90%+ of stocks. The other 90% feel the inflation, job anxiety, and credit stress. The market is not the economy. This divergence has never been wider. Conference Board prints at 10am today.
items.tweetB: Conference Board Consumer Confidence today. Implied SPX move plus or minus 0.84%. A print below 90 is flagged as a yellow light at current equity positioning. The context: the population already feels worse than during COVID shutdowns. There is no confidence cushion here. Watch how markets interpret the number vs the narrative.

items.title: South Korea Kospi hits all-time high on chip strength; Taiwan overtakes India as 5th largest stock market
items.idea: South Korea's Kospi surged 2.6% to a fresh all-time high after being closed Monday, driven by semiconductor strength on the NVIDIA earnings beat and Lenovo's 84% AI revenue growth. Japan's Nikkei also hit an ATH. Taiwan's equity market overtook India's to become the 5th largest stock market in the world — a direct consequence of the AI capital cycle flowing through TSMC, Samsung, and Hynix. The MSCI Asia All-Country index traded at a record high.
items.angle: The semiconductor complex is rewiring global equity market rankings in real time. Taiwan at number 5 is a structural consequence of AI capex, not a cyclical trade. Korea and Taiwan are where the AI infrastructure narrative is showing up in market cap weight.
items.tweetA: South Korea Kospi hit an all-time high today. Japan Nikkei also at ATH. Taiwan just became the 5th largest stock market in the world, passing India. AI capex is showing up in the market cap ranking of nations. TSMC, Samsung, Hynix are rewriting global equity weights. The AI trade is a geopolitical rebalancing. $EWY $EWT
items.tweetB: Taiwan passing India as the 5th largest market is the most underreported macro event of the week. This is not a cyclical blip. This is the permanent repricing of countries that own the manufacturing layer of the AI buildout. Foundries, memory, advanced packaging — Taiwan and Korea own all of it. This ranking gap will widen.

items.title: BTC ETFs bleed $2.26B in 2 weeks — worst stretch of 2026; HYPE ETF sees inflows; HYPE up 101% YTD vs BTC -12%
items.idea: US-listed spot Bitcoin ETFs saw $2.26 billion in outflows over the past two weeks — the worst two-week stretch of 2026. ETH ETFs are in similar shape. But Bitwise's BHYP (Hyperliquid ETF) and 21Shares' XRPZ are seeing positive inflows. Hyperliquid protocol generated $255M YTD in revenue with 97% flowing back to token holders through automated market purchases. HYPE is up 101% YTD against BTC's -12%.
items.angle: When new ETFs see positive flows against established products actively bleeding, it is the underlying thesis winning. The rotation from BTC/ETH to HYPE/XRP is the most interesting intra-crypto positioning signal right now. 97% revenue return to holders is a yield argument in a zero-yield market.
items.tweetA: Two-week BTC ETF outflow: $2.26B — worst stretch of 2026. Meanwhile HYPE ETF is seeing inflows and the protocol is up 101% YTD vs BTC -12%. Hyperliquid returned 97% of its $255M in 2026 revenue back to token holders via automated buybacks. This is what yield from protocol ownership looks like. $HYPE $BTC
items.tweetB: Saylor bought bonds last week. Not BTC. BTC ETFs are bleeding at a 2026-record pace. The capital is rotating into HYPE (protocol revenue model) and XRP (regulatory clarity from the Clarity Act). The cycle is maturing: narrative-driven speculative BTC is losing ground to fundamental protocol revenue stories. Watch the ETF flows.

items.title: Strategy bought bonds not BTC last week; BTC saw largest weekly ETF outflow of 2026
items.idea: Strategy (formerly MicroStrategy) purchased bonds rather than Bitcoin last week. BTC ETFs saw their largest weekly outflow of 2026. Fear and Greed index at 33. BTC at $76,810 (-1%), ETH at $2,099 (-1%), SOL at $85 (-1%). Bitcoin dominance held flat at 60%, suggesting the altcoin rotation is a reallocation within crypto, not an exodus from it.
items.angle: Strategy not buying BTC when the price dips is a behavioral signal. Saylor has been the reflexive buyer at virtually every price level for three years. A bond purchase combined with record ETF outflows suggests even the most committed BTC bulls are pausing.
items.tweetA: Strategy bought bonds last week. Not BTC. This is the same firm that has bought BTC at every price for three years. Combined with the largest BTC ETF weekly outflow of 2026, something has shifted in the conviction dynamic. Fear and Greed at 33. BTC at $77k. The reflexive buyer paused. Take note.
items.tweetB: BTC at $77k with the largest ETF outflow week of 2026 and Strategy pivoting to bonds is a combination that should not be dismissed. The bull case requires ongoing institutional inflows. If the most committed institutional buyer pivots to bonds, the marginal buyer question becomes much harder to answer. $BTC $MSTR

items.title: Stablecoin market cap $318B — now exceeds FX reserves of 95 nations
items.idea: The total stablecoin market value has reached $318 billion, exceeding the foreign exchange reserves of 95 countries. A technology less than a decade old has built a monetary base larger than most sovereign treasury systems. Tether and Circle now sit between the US dollar and much of the developing world as private financial intermediaries operating outside the traditional central bank framework.
items.angle: $318B in stablecoins is monetary colonization by private US dollar instruments. The Fed does not control this dollar supply. The FDIC does not insure it. 95 countries' populations are living in a parallel financial system. This is dollar hegemony 2.0 — run by private companies.
items.tweetA: The stablecoin market hit $318 billion — larger than the FX reserves of 95 countries. This is not a crypto statistic. This is a monetary system statistic. Tether and Circle have built a global dollar infrastructure that the US government neither controls nor fully understands. $USDT $USDC
items.tweetB: 95 nations have less FX reserves than the stablecoin market cap. Their citizens can access dollar-denominated savings through a phone app. Their central banks cannot stop it. The IMF is not equipped to regulate it. This is monetary disruption at sovereign scale and it is happening quietly.

items.title: Hyperliquid replaces oracles with validators for prediction markets — first market May CPI; $255M revenue 97% returned to holders
items.idea: Hyperliquid expanded its HIP-4 outcome market system so validators — not external oracles — handle deployment and settlement of prediction contracts tied to offchain events. Markets published by automated newsfeed software validators run as part of regular chain operations. First canonical market: May CPI year-over-year. This directly challenges Polymarket's UMA oracle model and moves resolution trust from a third party to the validator set already securing the chain. ICE and NYSE lobbied against Hyperliquid on the tokenized stocks front this week and won.
items.angle: Validator-based resolution is architecturally more trust-minimized than oracle-based because validators have economic stake in the chain. If a validator resolves incorrectly, their stake is at risk. Polymarket's UMA model has no such direct stake alignment.
items.tweetA: Hyperliquid just replaced oracles with validators for prediction markets. First market: May CPI. No UMA. No third party. Validators who secure the chain resolve outcomes — with their stake at risk. This is the architecture Polymarket cannot replicate. And ICE + NYSE proved this week they know it. $HYPE
items.tweetB: Validator settlement for prediction markets is the most elegant trust model in crypto right now. Polymarket uses a committee. Hyperliquid uses the validators already economically bonded to the chain. Wrong resolution equals stake at risk. Correct resolution equals revenue share. $255M revenue YTD, 97% returned to HYPE holders.

items.title: SEC reverses tokenized stocks in 72 hours — issuer-backed moving forward, third-party framework shelved indefinitely
items.idea: Three days after Bloomberg Law reported the SEC leaning toward allowing third-party tokenized stocks, the agency quietly shelved the framework indefinitely. ICE and NYSE lobbied hard and the SEC cited their concerns about dividend administration, shareholder voting, and investor protection. The result: issuer-backed tokenization (DTCC July launch, BlackRock and JPMorgan filings, Franklin Templeton) moves forward. Third-party workarounds like Kraken xStocks, OKX perps, and Hyperliquid pre-IPO contracts remain in legal gray territory.
items.angle: Legacy exchanges won a regulatory battle against DeFi. The decision tells you everything about regulatory capture: the tokenization version that helps incumbents gets the greenlight, the version that disrupts incumbents gets blocked. Innovation wins eventually, but incumbents bought themselves time.
items.tweetA: The SEC reversed its tokenized stocks framework in 72 hours. Bloomberg said it was moving forward Friday. By Monday it was shelved. ICE and NYSE lobbied against it and won. The reason: third-party tokenization threatens exchange revenue models. Issuer-backed still moves forward — through channels incumbents control.
items.tweetB: Two-track tokenization outcome: the version that helps incumbents gets regulatory greenlight. The version that disrupts incumbents gets blocked. This is textbook regulatory capture. Disruption will win eventually. But the timeline just got extended by a successful lobbying campaign from ICE and NYSE.

items.title: Vitalik shrinks EF mandate to CROPS — researcher departures rattle community; EF to sell less ETH
items.idea: Vitalik published the CROPS framework (Censorship resistance, Open source, Privacy, Security) as the new minimal mandate for the Ethereum Foundation, explicitly narrowing its scope. The EF will sell less ETH and retreat from broader coordination. Multiple Ethereum researchers have departed recently. The Ethereum Community Foundation was launched independently to fill the coordination gap.
items.angle: CROPS is principled but narrower coordination means slower evolution. Solana and Hyperliquid are shipping features aggressively during this Ethereum governance transition. Watch who fills the coordination void.
items.tweetA: Vitalik just narrowed the Ethereum Foundation to four things: Censorship resistance, Open source, Privacy, Security. CROPS. The EF will sell less ETH and retreat from coordination. Researchers are departing. A new Ethereum Community Foundation launches to fill the gap. This is the most important governance moment for Ethereum in two years. $ETH
items.tweetB: The EF retreating during the Solana and Hyperliquid offensive is either principled decentralization or dangerous abdication. CROPS is a strong framework. But Solana is shipping features weekly and Hyperliquid is building prediction markets, exchange infrastructure, and ETF products. Principled frameworks do not win market share races.

items.title: EIP-8182: native Ethereum private transfers pitched for Hegota upgrade — fewer than 1 in 10,000 ETH txns were private in 2025
items.idea: Tom Lehman (Facet co-founder) pitched EIP-8182 for inclusion in Ethereum's upcoming Hegota upgrade — a protocol-managed shielded pool for native private ETH and ERC-20 transfers. UTXO-based, no admin key, no proxy, no pause mechanism. Proofs via Groth16 BN254. Transfers work to any Ethereum address or ENS name with no privacy-specific format required. Fewer than 1 in 10,000 Ethereum transactions were private in 2025.
items.angle: If EIP-8182 makes it into Hegota, Ethereum becomes the first L1 with native protocol privacy. The ZEC and ZK privacy token narrative would reset if Ethereum absorbs the privacy stack natively.
items.tweetA: Fewer than 1 in 10,000 Ethereum transactions were private in 2025. EIP-8182 wants to fix that — native protocol-managed shielded pool for ETH and ERC-20 transfers, no third-party tools required. If this makes it into the Hegota upgrade, Ethereum absorbs the privacy stack natively. $ZEC $ETH
items.tweetB: EIP-8182: UTXO-based, no admin key, no pause, Groth16 proofs, works with any Ethereum address. What if Zcash-level privacy was native to Ethereum rather than bolted on? 1 in 10,000 private transactions is not a privacy network. This proposal is trying to change the baseline from opt-in to default.

items.title: TrapDoor malware: 34 packages across npm/PyPI/Crates.io targeting Solana/Sui/Aptos devs — modifies CLAUDE.md files to poison AI coding context
items.idea: Socket Security flagged TrapDoor — a malware campaign via 34 malicious packages and 384 versions across npm, PyPI, and Crates.io. Targets: Aptos, Sui, and Solana developers. Payload steals SSH keys, wallet keystores, AWS credentials, GitHub tokens, browser login databases, and crypto wallet extension data. Persistence via systemd, cron, Git hooks, and — critically — modified .cursorrules and CLAUDE.md files specifically targeting AI coding assistants. Campaign started May 22.
items.angle: The CLAUDE.md vector is the novel and alarming part: attackers are specifically poisoning the instruction files that AI coding assistants read. This is not just credential theft — it is poisoning the AI context that generates future code. Supply chain attack 2.0.
items.tweetA: TrapDoor malware hit npm, PyPI, and Crates.io targeting Solana, Sui, and Aptos devs. 34 packages, 384 versions. It steals SSH keys, wallet keystores, and AWS credentials. And it establishes persistence by modifying CLAUDE.md files to poison AI coding assistant context. Supply chain attacks just evolved. Crypto dev teams: audit your Claude context files now.
items.tweetB: The most dangerous part of TrapDoor: it modifies .cursorrules and CLAUDE.md — the instruction files AI coding tools read before generating code. This is not just credential theft. It is turning your AI assistant into a persistent malicious code generator. The attack surface expanded the day we gave AI write access to our development environments.

items.title: Ondo Finance CEO Nathan Allman dies unexpectedly — $3.5B TVL; McKinsey digital assets head assumes CEO role
items.idea: Nathan Allman, founder and CEO of Ondo Finance, died unexpectedly. Allman founded Ondo in 2021 after Goldman Sachs' digital assets team and built it into one of the most institutionally credible RWA platforms — $3.5B TVL spanning tokenized Treasuries, equities, and the USDY stablecoin. Ian De Bode, formerly head of digital assets at McKinsey, has assumed the CEO role.
items.angle: In the RWA space, institutional trust is built on founder relationships and personal credibility with sovereign and pension LP bases. Leadership transition risk at a $3.5B TVL platform is not trivial. Watch how institutional partners respond over the next 30 days.
items.tweetA: Nathan Allman, founder and CEO of Ondo Finance, has died unexpectedly. Goldman Sachs to Ondo in 2021 to $3.5B TVL in tokenized Treasuries and equities. He built one of the most institutionally credible RWA platforms in crypto. McKinsey digital assets head Ian De Bode takes over. The project continues. The founder is irreplaceable. $ONDO
items.tweetB: Ondo Finance loses its founder. The RWA institutional trust chain runs: Goldman DNA founder, credible pedigree, sovereign LP relationships, $3.5B TVL. That chain starts with the founder. McKinsey digital assets head replacing Goldman digital assets founder is a credibility transition, not just a management one. Watch institutional signals over 30 days.

items.title: India, Indonesia, Spain block prediction markets; US DOJ sues Minnesota over its ban — unprecedented regulatory divergence
items.idea: India cracked down on Polymarket and Kalshi this month. Indonesia blocked Polymarket (classifying it as gambling after a $46K bet on President Prabowo). Spain blocked access. Meanwhile the US Justice Department is suing Minnesota over its state-level ban on prediction markets. The asymmetry: every major democracy is banning prediction markets as gambling while the US federal government is suing a US state to protect them.
items.angle: The DOJ suing Minnesota to protect prediction markets is the most underreported regulatory development in crypto. If the DOJ wins, federal preemption of state-level bans becomes precedent — massive for Polymarket, Kalshi, and Hyperliquid's canonical markets.
items.tweetA: India blocked Polymarket and Kalshi. Indonesia blocked Polymarket. Spain blocked Polymarket. Minnesota banned prediction markets. And the US DOJ just sued Minnesota over that ban. Every major democracy cracking down while the US federal government fights to keep them open. This regulatory divergence will define the sector for a decade.
items.tweetB: The DOJ suing Minnesota to protect prediction markets is the most important regulation story nobody is talking about. If the federal government wins, state-level prediction market bans become unconstitutional. The market for information just got a potential constitutional protection layer. $HYPE Polymarket Kalshi all win if federal preemption holds.

items.title: Jack Clark at Oxford: AI went bar exam to IMO gold to novel proofs in 3 years — this cannot be treated as a normal technology
items.idea: In his 2026 Oxford Cosmos HAI Lab Lecture, Jack Clark walks through the Epoch Capabilities Index — 40+ benchmarks showing a vertigo-inducing upward curve. Key milestones: AI passed the bar exam (March 2023), silver medal at International Math Olympiad (July 2024), then gold (July 2025), co-authored novel mathematical proofs (2025), and systems like Claude Mythos are finding novel software flaws. His thesis: the underlying technology is common and improves predictably with compute and data. AI cannot be treated as a normal technology. The choice: explore the future or retreat from the present.
items.angle: Companies treating AI as a productivity tool are retreating from the present. Companies building capabilities are exploring the future. The investors who understand the pace — bar exam to IMO gold in 24 months — will be positioned differently than those still thinking in chatbot ROI terms.
items.tweetA: Jack Clark at Oxford: AI went from bar exam (2023) to IMO silver (July 2024) to IMO gold (July 2025) to co-authoring novel proofs in 2025. All from the same underlying technology that keeps improving with compute and data. This is not a productivity tool. This is a civilization-level technology. Explore the future or retreat from the present — there is no middle option.
items.tweetB: The Epoch Capabilities Index shows 40+ benchmarks all going up, on a curve that keeps steepening. Clark's point: the FOREST is growing faster than the individual trees. IMO gold was the most famous tree last year. What happens when the forest becomes the entire landscape? We are planning for success, not preparing for failure.

charts.desc: Charts inside — ECI graph worth opening

items.title: RSI replacing AGI as the 2027 benchmark — DeepSeek raises $10B prioritizing research over commercialization
items.idea: AI Supremacy argues that with AGI not commercially meaningful, Recursive Self-Improving AI (RSI) becomes the concrete next benchmark. 2027 is positioned as the RSI entry threshold, following three expected major AI IPOs in 2026. DeepSeek is raising 70 billion yuan ($10B) and explicitly tells investors it is prioritizing groundbreaking research over short-term commercialization. Demis Hassabis says today's systems are nowhere near AGI and is an angel investor in Anthropic. DeepMind alumni startups have collectively raised $14B+ since 2021.
items.angle: The shift from AGI to RSI as the primary benchmark is significant for investment thesis construction. RSI has a more concrete value proposition: AI improving chip design, protein folding, materials discovery in recursive loops. If 2027 is the threshold, the 2026 AI IPO window is designed to capture pre-RSI valuations.
items.tweetA: AGI is no longer a benchmark anyone serious takes at face value. The new benchmark is RSI — Recursive Self-Improving AI. DeepSeek just raised $10B and told investors they are prioritizing research to get there. The year: 2027. Three AI IPOs expected in 2026, designed to price before RSI changes the calculus entirely. The race has a new finish line.
items.tweetB: Demis Hassabis: today's AI is nowhere near AGI. He is also an angel investor in Anthropic. DeepMind alumni have raised $14B+ since 2021. The people declaring AGI far away are the same people profiting from the AI buildout that continues regardless. This is not hypocrisy — it is the business model.

items.title: OpenAI $6B Q1 revenue; Goldman: AI causing 16,000 US job losses per month
items.idea: OpenAI generated $6B in Q1 2026 revenue, maintaining its lead over Anthropic. Goldman Sachs separately estimated AI is now causing approximately 16,000 US job losses per month. Two sides of the same transformation: AI revenue accelerating at enterprise scale while labor market displacement begins showing up in statistical data rather than theoretical projections. 192,000 jobs per year at current run rate.
items.angle: The gap between AI revenue acceleration and job displacement statistics is where political risk lives. 16K jobs/month is too small to dominate policy yet too large to ignore. When this reaches 50-100K/month (likely by 2027), the political response changes character.
items.tweetA: OpenAI: $6B in Q1 2026 revenue. Goldman: AI causing 16,000 US job losses per month. Both are accelerating. One company captures the gains. 16,000 people per month absorb the losses. The economy will rebalance over time — but the distribution question is the political risk nobody in tech is pricing into their long-term thesis.
items.tweetB: 16K AI job losses per month. Goldman's current estimate. Goldman is also one of the firms most aggressively deploying AI to cut headcount. The analysts writing the displacement report are writing about themselves. At 192K per year current run rate, when does this reach political-shock territory? Probably when it hits 50K per month. We are not there. Yet.

items.title: Trump killed AI safety testing executive order hours before signing — after calls from Musk, Zuckerberg, and Sacks
items.idea: Trump's AI safety testing executive order was killed hours before the scheduled signing ceremony after last-minute calls from Elon Musk, Mark Zuckerberg, and David Sacks. The order would have imposed mandatory safety testing requirements on AI models before deployment. Three of the most powerful technology executives in Trump's orbit collectively convinced the President to withdraw the order in under 24 hours.
items.angle: Three tech founders called a president out of an executive order on AI safety in under 24 hours. Clearest possible signal about the balance of power between the US AI industry and federal regulation. The EU is watching and will use this as justification for why the EU AI Act is necessary.
items.tweetA: Trump was about to sign an AI safety testing executive order. Then Musk, Zuckerberg, and Sacks called. Hours before the ceremony, it was killed. Three tech founders. One president. US AI policy is being written in phone calls from billionaires. The EU is watching and taking notes — their AI Act just became the world's de facto AI safety standard.
items.tweetB: The AI testing order was not cancelled because the policy was wrong. It was cancelled because three of the most powerful voices in Trump's network made calls within hours of a signing ceremony. No hearing. No comment period. No congressional input. This is not how regulation normally works. The speed and informality is itself the story.

items.title: HP +15.25%, NetApp +12.44%, Zoom +9.19% on AI hardware read-through; Take-Two guides 70% below EPS expectations
items.idea: HP Inc rose 15.25% on peer read-through from Lenovo's AI-related revenue jumping 84% and Dell's $43B AI server backlog. NetApp rose 12.44% on AI-driven enterprise storage spending and significant pricing increases. Zoom rose 9.19% after reporting Q1 revenue $1.24B (vs $1.22B estimate), EPS $1.55 (vs $1.42), raising full-year guidance, and authorizing $1B share repurchase, citing AI-driven growth. Take-Two fell 4.42% after guiding FY27 EPS at $0.55-0.75 vs analyst expectations of $2.43, and revenue $7.9-8.1B vs $9.13B.
items.angle: The AI infrastructure read-through is now hitting legacy hardware companies meaningfully. HP, NetApp, storage vendors benefiting from enterprise data center modernization. Take-Two's miss is the gaming cycle at peak GTA VI delay risk.
items.tweetA: Lenovo AI revenue up 84%. Dell: $43B AI server backlog. Read-through: HP +15%, NetApp +12%. The enterprise AI buildout is now a pricing lever for legacy hardware vendors. Storage pricing up, server demand accelerating, infrastructure modernization spreading. This is not just a hyperscaler trade anymore — it is the entire enterprise hardware stack. $HPQ $NTAP $DELL
items.tweetB: Take-Two guided FY27 EPS at $0.55-0.75 against the street's $2.43. That is a 70% miss. Revenue $7.9-8.1B vs $9.13B. GTA VI is the only thing that makes this company work and its delay risk is becoming the entire thesis risk. Meanwhile Zoom beat estimates, raised guidance, and bought back $1B of stock. $TTWO $ZM

charts.desc: 4 stock charts: HP, NetApp, Zoom, Take-Two — Zoom chart described as one of the most compelling

items.title: Marvell earnings Wednesday = real read on whether NVIDIA AI halo trade flows through the full semiconductor stack
items.idea: The Gamma Report flags Marvell Technology (MRVL) reporting Wednesday after the close as the dominant AI earnings of the week — a direct read on AI chip demand downstream from NVIDIA's Wednesday beat. If Marvell guides down, the NVIDIA halo trade likely comes off. Marvell operates in the AI chip ecosystem below the GPU layer — networking chips, custom ASIC silicon, storage. Salesforce reports the same day as the enterprise AI spend bellwether.
items.angle: NVDA beats = everything in AI is working. MRVL guides down = NVDA is an island of strength, not a tide. The networking, custom silicon, and storage layers are the real signal on whether enterprise AI deployment is happening at scale.
items.tweetA: Marvell reports Wednesday. The Gamma Report calls it the dominant read on AI chip demand below the NVIDIA layer. If MRVL guides down after NVDA's beat last week, the conclusion: AI demand surge is not flowing through the full semiconductor stack. $MRVL is the canary for the AI capex trade. $NVDA $AVGO $MRVL
items.tweetB: NVDA beats: everything in AI is working. MRVL guides down: NVDA is an island, not a tide. The networking, custom silicon, and storage layers are the real signal on whether enterprise AI deployment is at scale. If Marvell is not seeing the AI demand wave, the hyperscaler buildout is not yet diffusing into the broader market.

items.title: Ukraine zero line year 4: infantry underground 6-9 months without sun; 2% casualties from small arms; all resupply by drone
items.idea: Rob Lee, reporting from Kyiv, describes the Ukrainian zero line in year four: infantry spending minimum 3 months, often 6-9 months, some over a year at position without rotation. Underground in basements or tree-line holes, minimal sunlight. All resupply by vampire drone (food, ammo, water, medical). CASEVAC takes 12 hours minimum — the golden hour is dead, tourniquets can sit on for a month. Only 2% of Ukrainian casualties come from small arms. Ukraine has reclaimed drone edge via Hornet, Bumblebee, and FP2 systems. New Azov-derived elite corps structure deployed. Starlink remains indispensable; Russia increasingly seriously jamming it.
items.angle: The 2% small arms casualty figure is the single most surprising data point in modern warfare. This reshapes procurement, doctrine, and what military readiness means for every NATO member.
items.tweetA: 2% of Ukrainian casualties come from small arms. Read that again. In historical warfare, small arms were the primary mechanism. In Ukraine year 4, it is noise. Drones, artillery, FPVs dominate. Infantry sit underground for 6-9 months without rotation, resupplied entirely by drone, with 12-hour CASEVAC windows. Modern war looks nothing like any training manual. $LMT $RTX
items.tweetB: Rob Lee from Kyiv: infantry walking 18-25km through covered routes just to exit the zero line. Tourniquets on for a month because CASEVAC takes 12 hours. Eyes adjusting back to daylight after 6 months underground. This is what the army receiving US military aid is actually experiencing in year 4. Context matters for every defense spending discussion.

items.title: Russia embeds hybrid warfare into Bauman Moscow State Technical University — Department 4 after Putin's Beijing visit
items.idea: Peter Frankopan, writing after Putin's visit to Beijing, reveals an investigation into Department 4 at Bauman Moscow State Technical University — Russia's equivalent of MIT. Russia is institutionalizing hybrid warfare by embedding it directly into higher education. Bauman has long produced engineers and coders for the Russian state. Frankopan's key observation: Russia understood something Western countries have struggled to grasp — information warfare and cyber disruption are graduate-level disciplines, not improvised activities.
items.angle: The China-Russia Beijing visit as context for a university-level hybrid warfare institutionalization story is the sequencing to read. The technology transfer and doctrine-sharing from China to Russia is likely more advanced than publicly disclosed.
items.tweetA: Russia is embedding hybrid warfare into Bauman Moscow State Technical University — their equivalent of MIT. Department 4. Not a shadow project. An institutional strategy. Frankopan writes this after Putin's Beijing visit. Russia's cyber and information warfare capability is graduate-level, institutionally funded, and expanding. This is what a peer adversary looks like.
items.tweetB: Frankopan's insight on Russia: wars are won in laboratories and classrooms, not just battlefields. Russia understood this. Most Western countries have not. Department 4 at Bauman is the physical evidence. Putin went to Beijing and returned to announce it. Read the sequencing carefully — the Beijing visit and the educational announcement are not unconnected.

items.title: Sulfur market triple-disrupted: Iran conflict + China protectionism + Ukraine drone attacks on Russia refineries all converge
items.idea: The sulfur market is simultaneously disrupted by three forces: the Iran conflict (Middle Eastern refineries heavily involved in sulfur trade), an unexpected protectionist move by China, and Ukraine's relentless attacks on Russia's refinery network. Sulfur was under $50/ton as recently as 2024. Its dominant downstream use is sulfuric acid, the primary input for phosphate-based fertilizers. Doomberg argues against catastrophizing — the disruptions are real but nuanced — however three simultaneous supply shocks on a single commodity with direct agricultural implications deserve serious attention.
items.angle: Sulfur is the most under-tracked commodity with direct agricultural implications. If all three disruptions sustain, fertilizer input costs ratchet up independently of oil price movements. Potential 2024-style food inflation replay through an unexpected vector.
items.tweetA: Sulfur was under $50 per ton in 2024. Now it faces three simultaneous supply disruptions: the Iran conflict hitting Middle Eastern refineries, China protectionism, and Ukraine drone strikes on Russian refinery networks. Sulfur makes sulfuric acid. Sulfuric acid makes phosphate fertilizers. If this triple shock holds, call your corn and wheat analysts. $MOS $NTR $CF
items.tweetB: Doomberg on sulfur: the catastrophizing is overblown but the triple supply disruption is real. Iran, China, Russia — three vectors on one commodity that feeds into the global food system. Sulfur was a nuisance byproduct at $50/ton. What it becomes in a world of simultaneous geopolitical supply shocks is a very different question.

charts.desc: Sulfur price history and supply chain analysis — Charts inside, worth opening

items.title: Hedge fund career ladder: Millennium accepted 190 of 50,000 applicants (0.38%); bonuses can go $400K to $0 in a single year
items.idea: Key structural facts from a comprehensive hedge fund career breakdown: Millennium accepted 190 interns from 50,000 applicants — 0.38% acceptance, lower than any Ivy League school. The classic 2/20 fee structure has compressed to approximately 1.5/15 average post-2008. 64% of US hedge fund assets are institutional (pensions, endowments). Lock-ups average 8 months. The high-water mark mechanic means analyst bonuses can legally go from $400K to $0 in a single year at the same fund. Summer intern base at Point72: approximately $100K annualized. Senior PM package at Millennium: up to $100M.
items.angle: The compressed fee structure combined with pass-through expense models at multi-manager pods creates extreme internal pressure. The 0.38% acceptance rate means talent is concentrated, but the economics per seat are deteriorating below the PM level — highly competitive entry with deteriorating payoff.
items.tweetA: Millennium accepted 190 interns from 50,000 applicants. That is 0.38% — lower than every Ivy League school. Classic 2/20 has compressed to 1.5/15. The high-water mark mechanic means analyst bonuses can go from $400K to $0 in a single year at the same fund. The hedge fund career has never been more competitive with deteriorating economics below PM level.
items.tweetB: The high-water mark is the most brutal mechanic in finance. A fund down 5% from peak owes no performance fee until it recovers. For analysts, bonuses can disappear entirely — not reduced, zero — because the firm has no profit to share. Meanwhile the fund manager still collects 1.5% of AUM in management fees. The risk is not symmetrically distributed.

items.title: Eli Lilly gene therapy may solve LDL cholesterol; Insilico building AI model for human longevity
items.idea: Eli Lilly is developing a gene therapy that could potentially resolve LDL cholesterol permanently rather than managing it chronically. Insilico Medicine is building an AI model for human longevity using a US partner to identify aging pathways and design molecules. The statin market generates approximately $20B annually. A permanent gene therapy solution would be an existential threat to that revenue stream.
items.angle: If Eli Lilly's LDL gene therapy works at scale, the chronic medication model for cardiovascular risk is disrupted permanently. The combination of Lilly's gene therapy and Insilico's AI longevity model represents the convergence of gene editing and AI drug discovery against aging — both accelerating simultaneously.
items.tweetA: Eli Lilly may have a gene therapy that solves LDL cholesterol. Not manages it. Solves it. The statin market is $20 billion per year. Pfizer, AstraZeneca, and Amgen make significant revenue from lipid-lowering drugs. A permanent gene therapy solution is an existential risk to that revenue model. Biotech is eating pharma from the inside. $LLY
items.tweetB: Insilico is building an AI model for human longevity — AI identifies aging pathways, designs molecules, runs digital trials. This is where AI drug discovery and the longevity thesis converge into a single accelerating curve. If it works, aging becomes an engineering problem with AI-compressed development timelines.

items.title: Catholic Church + Anthropic partner on AI path; Pope: AI needs to be disarmed
items.idea: The Catholic Church is reportedly teaming up with Anthropic on an AI path initiative. Simultaneously, the Pope declared that AI needs to be disarmed. This is the first major religious institution formally partnering with a frontier AI lab — the institution that once censured Galileo is now seeking a seat at the table for AI values frameworks. AI needs to be disarmed combined with formal partnership is the institutional version of if you cannot beat it, join it and shape the rules.
items.angle: 1.3 billion Catholics. One AI company. The Vatican and Anthropic partnership is the most important values alignment development of the month — not because it changes the technology but because it changes who gets a seat at the table when AI systems are given normative frameworks.
items.tweetA: The Catholic Church is partnering with Anthropic on an AI initiative. The Pope says AI needs to be disarmed. These two statements coexist in the same week from the same institution. The Church that once persecuted Galileo is now trying to shape the values of systems that may surpass human scientific capability. This is strategic engagement, not opposition.
items.tweetB: Vatican and Anthropic collaboration is the most significant AI values alignment development of May. Not because technology changes. Because the institution representing 1.3 billion people decided it needs a seat at the AI design table. If Anthropic shapes AI values with the Vatican, the output reaches every continent.

items.title: Nobu documentary: 10 months per year visiting every restaurant — the frontline obsession model for global empire building
items.idea: HardFork reviews the Nobu documentary about Nobuyuki Matsuhisa's culinary empire. Key business insight: Nobu spends 10 months per year traveling globally to personally visit every restaurant, maintaining culture and standards far from HQ. If you run a business or large organization, you have to visit the frontline all the time. That is where the reality lies. His origin story: father died young, adventurous spirit, apprenticed in Tokyo, moved to Lima Peru where he fused Japanese with South American ingredients, then Los Angeles, then global expansion. I started thinking food is like fashion.
items.angle: The 10-months-of-travel discipline is the explicit rejection of remote management. Nobu built a global empire through radical personal presence at the point of production — a trade-off increasingly relevant as remote-first companies struggle with culture dilution.
items.tweetA: Nobu spends 10 months per year visiting every restaurant in his global culinary empire personally. He started as a Tokyo sushi apprentice, moved to Lima Peru, fused cultures, and built one of the most valuable restaurant brands in the world. Management lesson: the coal face is where reality lives. Everything else is a story you tell yourself at HQ.
items.tweetB: I started thinking food is like fashion — Nobu. This one reframe built a global empire. Every category becomes something else when you find the right analogy before the disruption arrives. What does healthcare look like when it is software? What does banking look like when it is a protocol? The founder who finds the analogy first wins the decade.

items.title: Privacy x AI token down 97% from peak — partnered with Venice AI; Zeneca watching for next altcoin narrative rotation
items.idea: Zeneca identifies an unnamed token down 97% from its all-time high that sits at the intersection of AI and privacy, recently partnered with Venice AI for privacy infrastructure. The context: the current altcoin cycle is running hard on AI and privacy narratives — NEAR up 77% in one week, WLD up 60%, VVV, DIEM, ZEC, SERV all running. The token has been building a real product while its price has been crushed.
items.angle: The deepest value in every altcoin cycle is always in the last name to move in the dominant narrative. AI x Privacy is the thesis. The infrastructure-layer tokens with real product development and 97% drawdowns from ATH are where asymmetric risk lives when the narrative is already established.
items.tweetA: NEAR up 77% in a week. WLD up 60%. VVV, ZEC, SERV running. The AI x Privacy altcoin narrative is live. Zeneca is watching a token down 97% from peak, building real infrastructure, partnered with Venice AI. In every altcoin cycle, the deepest value is the last name to move in the dominant narrative. Infrastructure always moves after the front-end tokens.
items.tweetB: Down 97% from peak. Real product. Venice AI partnership. AI x Privacy intersection. No price run yet. This is either a value trap or the asymmetric setup in the hottest current altcoin narrative. The names that are building while the market ignores them tend to catch up violently when the rotation finally arrives.

items.title: Tether + Government of Georgia launch GELT (Lari stablecoin) — first sovereign government-aligned Tether stablecoin
items.idea: Tether is partnering with the government of Georgia to launch GELT, a Georgian Lari-denominated stablecoin. This is Tether's first formal sovereign government co-issued stablecoin, creating a precedent for central bank-adjacent Tether partnerships at the national level. For Georgia, it means digital currency infrastructure without CBDC development costs. For Tether, it means sovereign legitimacy and a template that exports to any emerging market wanting digital currency rails without building from scratch.
items.angle: The Tether and Georgia model inverts the CBDC question entirely. Instead of a government building a digital currency, a government co-brands an existing private stablecoin infrastructure. If this model succeeds, Tether's distribution moat gains a sovereign endorsement playbook that can be replicated across 50+ emerging markets.
items.tweetA: Tether and the government of Georgia are launching GELT — a Lari-denominated stablecoin. First formal sovereign government Tether partnership. If this works, the playbook exports to every emerging market that wants digital currency infrastructure without the CBDC build cost. Tether just found the sovereign distribution model. $USDT
items.tweetB: The Tether and Georgia model is the smartest fintech play of the year. Government co-brands private stablecoin. Tether gets sovereign legitimacy. Georgia gets instant digital currency rails. No CBDC development cost. No monetary sovereignty lost beyond what dollarization already costs. Every EM finance minister just got handed a template.
